title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms / Chapter 2 — Zhang Fei Thrashes a Government Inspector / He Jin Secretly Plots to Kill the Eunuchs
text:
Let us turn now to thisDong Zhuo. He, styledZhongying, was fromLintao county,Longxi commandery. Ever since the government had conferred upon him the rank ofGrand AdministratorofHedong, he had become arrogant and overbearing. The disdain he showed this day towardsXuanderoused the anger ofZhang Fei, who was for killing him atonce.1•••

Longxi was in the far northwest, in Liang province.↩

YetXuandeandLord Guanquickly stopped him, saying, “He has received his orders from the imperial court. Who are we to kill him?”

Zhang Feisaid, “But if I do not kill this wretch, I will have to serve under him and take his orders instead. That would never suit my tastes! Brothers, you may stay if you like, but I will leave this place!”

Xuandesaid, “We three are bound to share life and death. How could we part from one another? If you are leaving, we will accompany you.”

AndZhang Feireplied, “If that is the case, you have lowered my anger.” So the three of them led their troops away again, leaving that same night to return toZhu Jun. He was a much more gracious host, and he merged their troops into his own forces to advance and campaign againstZhang Bao.

By now,Cao CaoandHuangfu Songhad led their forces away to campaign againstZhang Liangand were locked in a great battle atQuyang, whileZhu Junremained behind to march againstZhang Bao.Zhang Baobrought up an army of eighty or ninety thousand rebels to camp behind a hill.Zhu JuncommandedXuandeto lead their vanguard, andXuandeled the soldiers forward to approach the enemy lines.Zhang Baoin turn sent his general,Gao Sheng, to ride forth and offer a duel, whileXuandeorderedZhang Feiforward to take up the challenge.Zhang Feibarreled forward at full speed, spear ready-set, and within a few bouts had runGao Shengthrough, felling him from his horse.Xuandethus signaled for his troops to charge.

Zhang Bao, from atop his mount, loosed his hair to hang freely, grasped his sword, and began conjuring a dark magic. Soon the wind began to howl and the thunder rolled as a dense black cloud formed from the heavens and settled upon the field, and from within there seemed to spring a countless host of murderous riders, throwing the army into confusion as they descend from the sky.Xuande, seeing this, called a retreat. The battle lost,Xuandereturned toZhu Junto report what hadhappened.•••

Maybe this is whatLu Zhiwas warning of in chapter one

Zhu Junsaid, “They are using the dark arts. Tomorrow, let us butcher sacrificial animals for their blood and command our troops to lie in ambush atop the hill. They will watch for the enemies to rush by and then douse them with blood from the heights, thus will their magic be dispelled.”

Xuandesaw to the preparations: he hadLord GuanandZhang Feieach take a thousand soldiers to conceal themselves atop tall ridges behind the hills and supplied them with plenty of sacrificial blood as well as various refuse from the animals.

The following day, whenZhang Baounfurled his banners and beat his drums as he brought his troops forward to offer battle,Xuandemarched out to face him. Once the two sides began to clash,Zhang Baoagain conjured his magic. Wind and lightning began to roar, sand flew in the breeze, pebbles were swept along the ground, and black masses of vapor filled the sky and rolling masses of foot and horse descended from on high.Xuandereined in his horse and fled, andZhang Baocommanded his troops onward in pursuit. Yet as they were passing by the hill, a signal-bomb went off, andLord GuanandZhang Fei’s ambush troops emerged, throwing down upon their enemy filth and splattering them with blood. The fearsome host soon proved to be no more than men of paper and horses of grass, tumbling one by one to the ground, while the wind ceased to blow, the thunder subsided, the sand sank, and the pebbles lay still upon the ground.

Seeing that his magic had been countered,Zhang Baoquickly tried to withdraw his army. Yet by now he was being attacked byLord Guanon the left andZhang Feion the right, whileXuandeandZhu Junhad turned back to chase him from behind, and the rebels were greatly defeated. Glimpsing the general’s banner for the “Lordly General of Earth” himself,Xuandegalloped forward to chase after him. YetZhang Baobeat a hasty retreat, and thoughXuandeshot an arrow at him and struck him in the left arm,Zhang Baogot away arrow and all; he fled into the city ofYangcheng, where he kept to his defenses and would not risk another fight.Zhu Junled the army to besiege that city, while sending off a scout to informHuangfu Songof thesituation.•••

Magic is not often used in battle in the novel and the Turbans resorting to such means are not a good indication on the Turbans

The scout soon returned, having completed his mission. He also reported, “SinceHuangfu Songhad won several great victories whileDong Zhuohad been suffering several defeats, the court orderedHuangfu Songto go take command of his army. Although by the timeHuangfu Songhad arrivedZhang Juewas already dead,Zhang Lianghad assumed leadership of his army and was still opposing our forces, yetHuangfu Songdefeated him in seven successive battles and took his head atQuyang. He also exhumedZhang Jue’s coffin, mutilated the corpse, and impaled his head, later delivering it to the capital. The remaining rebels have all surrendered. The court has promotedHuangfu Songto beGeneral of Chariots and Cavalryand actingGovernorofJi province.2•••

General of Chariots and Cavalry was second highest miliatry rank and head of Ji was placing him in charge of the province he had just pacified.↩

There was a popular song aboutHuangfu Songin Ji for bringing peace “When the nation became chaotic, cities turned into ruins. Mothers couldn’t protect their sons, and wives lost their husbands. Thanks to Lord Huangfu, we were able to live in peace”

“Huangfu Songalso presented a petition attesting thatLu Zhiachieved much and done no wrong, thus the court has restoredLu Zhito his former office.

“Cao Cao, also due to his achievements, was promoted asChancellorofJi’nan; on that day, he led his troops back to the capital to assume office.”

Hearing these things,Zhu Junordered an assault, turning the full strength of his army to attackYangcheng. With defeat imminent, one of the rebel generals,Yan Zheng, assassinatedZhang Baoand presented his head in an act of surrender.Zhu Junthus pacified several local commanderies before making an official report of his victories to the government.

***

However, embers of rebellion still smoldered. Three of theYellow Turbans—Zhao Hong,Han Zhong, andSun Zhong—mustered a new army of several tens of thousands and began to raid and plunder wherever they could, calling themselves the avengers ofZhang Jue.Zhu Junwas commanded by the court to lead his veteran troops on a campaign to destroy them.

By now some of the rebels had occupied the city ofYuan, soZhu Junmarched to confront them there.Zhao HongsentHan ZhongtoYuanto meetZhu Jun’s army.Zhu JunorderedXuandeand the brothers to attack the city from the southwest.Han Zhongrushed with his best soldiers to confront them there. Meanwhile,Zhu Junled two thousand armored horsemen to attack the city from the northeast. FearingYuanwould be lost,Han Zhongabandoned the battle at the southwest to meetZhu Jun’s forces in the northeast, butXuandepressed them from behind, throwing their forces into disarray. Badly defeated, the survivors fled into the city.Zhu Junthen divided his army to besiegeYuanfrom all sides. With food stores depleted and famine setting in,Han Zhongsent an agent to offer surrender.Zhu Junrefused.•••

Wan was in the old home of the Later Han imperial family and had become the unofficial third capital of the empire with an eye for fashion.

Xuandesaid, “In times past theSupreme Progenitor, founder of theHan dynasty, obtained the world most likely due to his ability to invite surrender and accept submission. Why then, sir, do you refuseHan Zhong?”3•••

Gaozu is theposthumous titleofLiu Bangthe founder of theHan.↩

“That was then,” repliedZhu Jun, “and this is now. In those days there was such disorder, between the fall of theQinand the machinations ofXiang Yu, that there was no consistent master whom the people were obliged to obey. Enticing surrender and rewarding defection was the only way others could be encouraged to come to one’s side. Yet in our time all the realm within the Four Seas has but one sovereign while only theseYellow Turbansare in rebellion; to tolerate their treason by permitting them to surrender is no way to encourage good conduct. Letting such brigands plunder as they please while they hold the advantage and then give themselves up once they lose it would only promote constant turmoil; it is a poorplan.”4•••

Xiang Yuwas involved in the fall of theQindynasty and the major opponent of the founder of theHanas he sought to establish his own hegemony.↩

“I will grant that we cannot let banditry go unpunished,” concededXuande, “but the city is surrounded as if by an iron barrel, and should the rebels’ request be refused, they must fight to the death. Where a myriad men fight with one heart, they cannot be withstood; wouldn’t that apply all the more when tens of thousands of men trapped in the city are resolved to die? We could withdraw from the east and south sides of the city while assaulting from the north and west. The rebels will abandon the city to flee. With their will to fight lost, we can capture themeasily.”•••

This is not just a debate on what to do with rebels but fear of forcing the Turbans onto “death ground” where, with no escape, the Turbans will fight desperately.

This convincedZhu Jun, who pulled back his troops from the south and east while assaulting the north and west. As predicted,Han Zhongled his soldiers to abandon the city and flee.Zhu Jun,Xuande,Guan Yu, andZhang Feiled their three armies to attack them.Han Zhongwas shot dead while the other rebels scattered in all directions. However, at this time,Zhao HongandSun Zhongarrived with their own armies and engagedZhu Jun.Zhu Junrecognized that they were too strong and pulled his troops back some distance.Zhao Hongexploited the opportunity to retake control ofYuan.•••

In the original much lengthier novel, it wasZhu Junwho personally shoots downHan Zhong.

Zhu Junencamped tenlifrom the city and prepared to attack. Just then, there arrived a force of cavalry and foot soldiers from the east, led by a remarkable general.

This man had a broad open face, he was lithe and yet powerfully built. He was a native ofFuchun countyinWu commandery, and his name wasSun Jian, styledWentai, a descendant ofSun Wu. At the age of seventeen, he had once traveled with his father along theQiantang Riverwhere they had spotted a party of pirates who, having just plundered the wares of merchants, were dividing their booty on the riverbank.5•••

Sun Wuis better known as Master Wu or Sun Tzu. Distant claims might be considered a family claim to ancient past rather then something provable. In fact, the history books do not explicitly callSun Wuthe ancestor ofSun Jian.↩

Sun Jiantold his father, “These bandits can be captured.”

Sword in hand,Sun Jianran boldly up the bank while calling out loudly and gesturing to his right and left as if directing his troops. Believing government soldiers were closing in on their position, the pirates abandoned their booty and fled.Sun Jianpursued them and even managed to kill one. This incident had won him a certain reputation throughout the county, and he was recommended for the post ofcommandant.

Later a mystic fromKuaiji commandery,Xu Chang, started a rebellion, gathering tens of thousands of men and calling himself theYangming Emperor.Sun Jiancollaborated with a localcommanding officerto recruit more than a thousand warriors and joined the regional provincial armies to defeat him. He took the heads ofXu Changand his son, Xu Hao. TheInspector,Zang Min, sent a petition to the court detailingSun Jian’s deeds, andSun Jianwas promoted to serve asDeputyatYandu,Xuyi, andXiapi.6•••

Xu Changwas one of a number of religious revolts in the south during the 170’s.↩

When theYellow Turban Rebellionbegan,Sun Jiangathered young men from his village, along with veteran soldiers and traveling merchants from regions along theHuaiandHuaiRivers—a force more than fifteen hundred in all—and marched to the battle front where he encounteredZhu Jun, embattled atFangcheng.

Overjoyed to receiveSun Jian’s aid,Zhu Junpromptly ordered him to attack the south gate of the city.Xuandewent to attack the north gate andZhu Junattacked the west, leaving the east gate left free to give the rebels a route of escape.Sun Jianwas the first to mount the wall where he personally cut down more than twenty men. The rebel army pulled back and fell into confusion. MeanwhileZhao Hong, spear at the ready to strike, rode directly forSun Jian, but he leaped from the wall, seizedZhao Hong’s spear, and skewered him with it, sending his body toppling from his horse. Mounting the horse,Sun Jianrode hither and thither, slaying many as he went.

Sun Zhongled the rebels to break out through the north gate, when they encounteredXuande’s soldiers they had no heart to fight and only attempted to get away.Xuandedrew his bow and loosed an arrow, strikingSun Zhong, who slumped over and fell from his horse. Then the main army underZhu Juncame up and fell upon the rebels from behind to complete the slaughter: they took tens of thousands of heads while countless other rebels surrendered. ThroughoutNanyangdozens of counties were pacified.

Zhu Junled his army back to the capital where he was promoted toGeneral of Chariots and Cavalryand madeGrand AdministratorofHenan. He too submitted a petition listing the achievements ofSun JianandLiu Bei.7•••

Henan was the area around the capital,Zhu Junwas in (partial) charge of security and supply for the capital.↩

Thanks to the connectionsSun Jianhad, he was appointed as acommanding officerand went to report to his new post. In contrast,Xuandewaited in vain for a response, never receiving an appointment, and the three brothers weremelancholy.8•••

Appointment due to connections is not a good sign of government.↩

One day, the brothers were walking down the street when the carriage of one of the palace cadets,Zhang Jun, approached.Xuandemet with him and told him of his noble achievements. Shocked,Zhang Junwent to court to meet Emperor Ling.•••

Zhang Junmay have been General of the Household and in more of a position to get involved.

“The source of theYellow Turbans’ rebellion,” he said, “was all because theTen Regular Attendantssold offices and bartered ranks; there was employment only for their friends, punishment only for their enemies. This drove the realm to rebellion. You ought to beheadThe Tenand hang up their heads outside the southern gate of the capital, while dispatching agents across the realm to announce that whoever has done good deeds for the state will receive their just rewards. Then all the land within the Four Seas would calm down of its own accord.”

The Tensubmitted their own petition arguing that: “Zhang Junis deceiving Your Majesty.” Emperor Ling ordered the guards to expelZhang Jun.•••

The paragraph shows how much influence the eunuchs have over the emperor; with barely any words and with practically no evidence, they convince the emperor to ignoreZhang Juncompletely.

YetThe Tenreasoned among themselves, “This must have been the result of people who, having not gotten any office for their deeds in fighting theYellow Turbans, have started grumbling. For now, let’s instruct the officials to draw up a list of obscure people; we can always revisit the matter lateron.”•••

In the original novel, Emperor Ling proposes looking for those overlooked after the Turban wars

ThusXuandeat last received an office, having been appointed as commandant ofAnxi countyin theZhongshan commanderyinDingzhouand assigned a date to report to his post. He ordered his troops to disperse back to their home villages, only retaining about twenty trustedfollowers.9•••

Zhongshan should be a commandery rather than a “military district”. There was no such province as Dingzhou (定州) at this time.↩

He then arrived atAnxialong withLord GuanandZhang Fei. For a whole month in office,Xuandenever made the slightest infringement against the people, who were moved and inspired by his restraint. And thoughLord GuanandZhang Feiate at the same table and slept on the same couch with him, wheneverXuandewas in the company of others the two younger brothers would stand at attendance, were it even a wholeday.10•••

Officials who were seen as able and virtuous would be said to have reformed their area by their own good example meaning others followed the example and reformed.↩

Less than four months afterXuande’s arrival, there came from the imperial court an edict calling for the reduction of local officials who had been appointed based on battlefield achievements, andXuandesuspected that he would be among those dismissed. Thus when theDistrict Inspectorcame toAnxi,Xuandeexited the city to meet him, and welcomed him with full honors. Yet theinspectorremained seated atop his horse, answering only with a flick of his whip.Lord GuanandZhang Feiseethed withrage.11•••

Remaining on top of one’s horse is considered very rude in Chinese custom.↩

When theinspectorhad arrived at his lodging, he took his seat facing south on the dais asXuanderemained standing at the foot of the stairs. After a long time theinspectoraddressed him.

“County CommandantLiu, what is your origin?”

Liu Beireplied, “I am descended fromPrince Jing of Zhongshan. Since I first campaigned against theYellow TurbansatZhuo county, I have been in more than thirty battles, large or small, from which I earned some trifling merit. My reward was this office.”

“You lie about your ancestry and your statement of services is false,” roared the. “I have here a decree calling for the elimination of corrupt officers and low-class officials such as yourself.”

Xuandeplacated him with mumbled agreements and withdrew. Upon returning to the county office, he asked thecounty officialsfor advice.

“This pompous attitude can only means he wants a bribe,” said he.

“Yet I have not deprived the people of even a single hair,” saidXuande. “I have nothing of value. What bribe could I possibly give?”

On the next day, theinspectorsummoned thiscounty officialand forced him to record thatCommandantLiuwas abusing the people.Xuandewent time after time to refute this charge, but on each occasion was driven away by the guards.

NowZhang Feihad spent the day drowning his sorrow in wine, and had drank far too much. Calling for his horse he rode past theinspector’s lodging, where at the gate he found fifty or sixty old people weeping bitterly. Upon asking why, they explained, “Theinspectorhas compelled thecounty officialto bear false witness againstLord Liu. We came to beg mercy, but were denied entry and beaten by the guards!”

This provoked the irascible and half intoxicated man to fury. His eyes bulged from their sockets; he ground his teeth; in a moment he was off his steed, had forced his way past the scared doorkeepers into the building and was in the rear apartments. There he saw theDistrict Inspectorsitting on high with the county officials in bonds on the ground.

Zhang Feiroared, “You thieving oppressor of the people! Do you know who I am?”

But before he could utter a wordZhang Feihad him by the hair and had dragged him down. Another moment he was outside and firmly lashed to a hitching post in front of the county office. Then breaking off switches from a willow tree,Zhang Feigave his victim a severe thrashing on the thighs, striking with such force that more than ten times the switch broke to pieces at a stroke.

Xuandewas sitting alone, communing with his sorrow, when he heard the commotion going on outside the county office. He asked some attendants what was happening, and they reported, “GeneralGuanhas bound somebody to a post in front of the office and is thrashing him.” Hastily going outside to look he saw that the man bound to the post was theinspector. Shocked,Liu Beidemanded an explanation.

Zhang Feisaid, “People like him are bandits who oppress the people. It would be worse not to beat them todeath!”•••

Zhang Feibeating the inspector was used by earlier plays as one of his great deeds to be boasted of.

Theexclaimed, “LordXuande, save my life!”

AsXuandewas a kind and benevolent man at heart, he orderedZhang Feito stay his hand at once.

ThenLord Guancame up besides him, saying, “Elder Brother, even after all your great achievements, you barely even got an appointment as a county commandant. Even here, you have been insulted by thisinspector. I think that a thorn bush is no place for a phoenix to perch. Let us slay this fellow, return back home, and make a new plan for greater things.”

Xuandethus hung his official seal about the neck of theDistrict Inspector, saying, “I should kill you for the harm you have done to the people, but I will show mercy by sparing your life. Here are my seal and ribbon of office. We are leaving.”

TheDistrict Inspectorwent and reported the incident to theGrand AdministratorofDingzhou, who forwarded the report to the military officials and dispatched agents to arrest the brothers. But the three of them went toDaizhouand took refuge withLiu Hui; sinceXuandewas a relative of theHanimperial family,Liu Huisheltered them in his home and did not report them.

***

By this time, having monopolized power and authority, theTen Regular Attendantsinvolved themselves in every discussion and mere disagreement could lead to execution.Zhao ZhongandZhang Rangsent agents to demand gold and silk from any general or officer who had put down theYellow Turbans, and for those who failed to comply, the duo presented petitions having them removed from office. SinceHuangfu SongandZhu Junwould not to give in to such bribes,Zhao Zhongand the rest petitioned for their dismissal. For his part, Emperor Ling appointedZhao Zhongand others asGeneral of Chariots and Cavalryand appointed thirteen people, includingZhang Rang, as landedRanged Marquis. The government descended deeper into corruption while the people howled with anger.

Thus a bandit inChangsha,Ou Xing, started causing trouble. At the same time, inYuyang,Zhang JuandZhang Chunrebelled, withZhang Judeclaring himself Son of Heaven andZhang Chunappointing himself asGrand General. Although petitions and reports flowed in like snowflakes detailing these emergencies,the Tenconcealed these reports rather than inform Emperor Ling.

One day Emperor Ling was at a feast in one of the rear gardens withthe Tenwhen one of theGrandee Remonstrant,Liu Tao, suddenly stood before Emperor Ling while sighing deeply.

The Emperor asked what was thematter.12•••

Ou Xingwas a revolt of ten thousand with alliances across Southern Jing in the south. Yuyang was in You province in northeast bordering Wuhuan lands. Revolts were spreading north and south.↩

Liu Taowas a descendant of the Former Han Imperial Family with a bluff manner, been active at court affairs even as a long time student. An active critic in the government, he was rewarded for forewarning about the Turbans.

Liu Taosaid, “Sire, how can you be feasting with these eunuchs when the Empire is at its last gasp?”

The Emperor said, “The empire is at peace. What danger is there that is so pressing?”

Liu Taosaid, “Robbers are swarming on all sides and plundering the provinces and commanderies. This disaster is all the fault of theTen Regular Attendants, who sell offices, injure the people, cheat their lord, and deceive Your Majesty. All virtuous men in the court have left and disaster is before our very eyes!”

At this,The Tenpulled off their hats and threw themselves at the feet of the Emperor.

They said, “When such a great minister disapproves of us, how can we expect to survive? If only you would spare our lives and permit us to return to our fields and villages, we would yield all our property to help defray the military expenses.”

And having spoken, they wept bitterly. The Emperor turned toLiu Taoand fumed, “You also have servants! Why is it that you cannot tolerate mine?”

He called to the guards to ejectLiu Taoand behead him. ButLiu Taocried, “I die without any regrets! But how pitiful that theHan, after more four centuries of rule, will end overnight!”

The guards hustled him away and were just about to carry out their orders when another important minister called out to stop them, saying, “Strike not! Wait until I have remonstrated with His Majesty.”

The guards saw that it was theExcellency over the Masses,Chen Dan. He went in to the Emperor to make his objection, saying, “For what crime isCounselorLiuto be put to death?” And the Emperor said, “He has vilified my servants and insultedme.”13•••

Chen Danhad been in and out of office through the 170’s and 180’s, and was known for his honesty and loyalty.↩

ButChen Dansaid, “The whole world would eat the flesh ofthe Tenif they could, and yet, Your Majesty, you respect them as if they were your parents, and though they have not one inch of merit, you have made them landed nobles. Even worse,Feng Xuand the others were in league with theYellow Turbansand planned to cause chaos within the capital. Since Your Majesty is not taking this seriously, the empire will be ruined!”

The Emperor said, “There was no proof thatFeng Xumeant to cause trouble. Are there none faithful amongthe Ten?”

Chen Danbegan to beat his forehead on the steps of the throne to protest further. Emperor Ling grew angry and commanded his removal, having him imprisoned along withLiu Tao. That night,the Tenarranged to have them murdered inprison.•••

Liu Taowas said to be widely mourned.

Then a forged edict went forth makingSun JiantheGrand AdministratorofChangsha, with orders to suppressOu Xing’s rebellion. In less than fifty days he reported that he had triumphed and that the region aroundJiangxiawas pacified. For this he was createdMarquisofWucheng.

Meanwhile,Liu Yuwas madeGovernorofYou provinceand led troops againstYuyangto suppressZhang JuandZhang Chun.Liu HuiofDaizhouwrote a letter recommendingXuandeto toLiu Yu, who welcomed him as hisChief Commandant.Xuandeled troops straight towards the rebel lair where he fought a great battle against them for several days, blunting their resolve and sapping their morale untilZhang Chun’s violence and cruelty lost him the support of his soldiers. One of his chieftains stabbed him to death, then surrendered both his old master’s head and the remainder of the army toLiu Bei.Zhang Ju, realizing all was lost, hangedhimself.14•••

MinisterLiu Yuwas an imperial relative with a reputation for kindness and virtue. He had governed in the area before and adopted their clothing to win support, also popular with the Wuhuan who were backing the Zhang’s.↩

Yuyangbeing now tranquil,Liu YureportedLiu Bei’s services to the throne. Not only was he pardoned for whipping theInspector, he was appointed asDeputyofXiami, and then asCounty CommandantofGaotang.Gongsun Zanalso reportedXuande’s previous good services, and recommended that he be appointed as an independent marshal and as actingChiefofPingyuan.15While atPingyuan,Xuandewas able to obtain cash and supplies, men and horses, and recovered something of his old manner before the days of adversity.Liu Yualso received preferment for his suppression of the rebellion and was promoted toGrand Commandant.16•••

Gongsun Zan, as well as beingLiu Bei’s old classmate, was an experienced commander against the Wuhuan in the northeast↩

Grand Commandant putLiu Yuas the most senior Excellency↩

***

In thesixth year of Central Peace (189 AD), at the beginning of summer, Emperor Ling became seriously ill and summoned theGrand General,He Jin, into the palace to arrange for the future. Although thisHe Jinhad sprung from a humble family of butchers, after his sister had entered the palace harem he attained prominence, and once she gave birth to the Emperor’s son, Liu Bian, and was appointedEmpress,He Jinwielded considerable power and held high office. Emperor Ling had had another favorite, Beauty Wang, who had likewise given birth to his sonLiu Xie, butEmpress Hewas a jealous woman and had used poison to kill Beauty Wang.Liu Xiehad thus been raised within the palace by Empress Dowager Dong, the mother of Emperor Ling and the widow of theDistrict MarquisofJiedu district,Liu Chang. The late Emperor Huan had had no sons of his own and had resorted to adoptingLiu Chang’s son, and when the boy later became Emperor Ling he brought his mother’s family into the palace with him and honored her as Empress Dowager.17•••

Lady Wangwas from a more notable, respectable family thenEmpress He. She was considered attractive and clever, literate and skilled in math.↩

The He’s were the first Empress family outside a select circle in the Later Han though the He’s were from a traditional choice of place for an imperial bride in Nanyang.

Harem politics could be brutal, the loss of favor for an Empress could get them killed and a rival child to one’s own made a rival woman even more of a threat. However the inner palace could also be suspect to salacious gossip and suspicion when deaths did happen

Emperor Ling wrote poems about the late Beauty Wang.

Empress Dowager Dong had sought to persuade her son to nameLiu Xieas the Heir Apparent, and in fact Emperor Ling greatly loved the boy and was disposed to do as his mother desired. Thus when he fell ill, one of the eunuchs,Jian Shuo, said to him “If you wantLiu Xieto succeed you,He Jinmust be killed first to remove any future trouble.” The Emperor saw this too and commandedHe Jinto come to him within the palace. Yet whenHe Jinreached the palace gates, one of the majors,Pan Yin, warned him, “You can’t go in there;Jian Shuois plotting to kill you.” Alarmed,He Jinswiftly retreated back to his own residence, where he called the chief ministers to his side to consider how to put all the eunuchs todeath.18•••

He Jinas brother to the Empress and in control of the Northern Army (the capital defense force and the only professional army in the land) would have both political and miliatry authority to help his nephew to the throne should Emperor Ling die.↩

Jian Shuowas noted for his strength and military planning, he was technicallyHe Jin’s superior and in charge of the newly formed Western Garden Army

One man drew himself up to declare, “The eunuchs have wielded power ever since the reigns of emperorsChongandZhi, and now their influence spreads throughout the court like a noxious weed. How could we bring about their total execution? If our secret ever got out, we would each suffer the exterminations of our clan. You had better consider thiscarefully.”1920•••

The novel is dating the eunuchs rise to the two child Emperors of 145–156 under the regency ofLiang Ji.↩

Pan Yinwas an old friend ofHe Jin↩

He Jinlooked to see who had spoken; it wasCao Cao, who by now was serving in the capital asCommandant for Military Standards.He Jinsneered, “How can whelps like you have any understanding of the great affairs of court?”

The discussion was still going whenPan Yinarrived to inform them, “The Emperorhas just passed away. However,Jian Shuoand theTen Regular Attendantsare talking among themselves. They mean to keep the death a secret and to forge an edict summoningState Uncle Heinto the palace to remove any future threats. They have already marked downLiu Xieas the newEmperor.” He had not finished speaking when a messenger appeared, orderingHe Jinto enter the palace at once to settle thesuccession.•••

For the death of an Emperor, it is almost just a side moment of the story.

Cao Caosaid, “We must first settle the succession. The traitors can be dealt with later.”

He Jinproclaimed, “Who dares help me acclaim our true lord and eliminate the traitors?”

At once another man put himself forward, saying, “Give me five thousand veterans and we will break into the palace, set up the true heir, slay all the eunuchs, and sweep clean the government in order to settle the realm!”

He Jinsaw that this was theColonel who Directs Retainers,Yuan Shao, styledBenchu; he was the son of the formerExcellency over the Masses,Yuan Feng, and the nephew ofYuan Wei.21•••

Yuan Shaowas from one of the two greatest families in the land, the richest family in the capital and with high ranks almost by right.Yuan Weias grand tutor with charge of sectariat held important role↩

Yuan Shaohad run escape lines for eunuch opponents so was already a noted opponent

Delighted by this request,He Jinassigned five thousand of the palace guards toYuan Shao’s command, whileYuan Shaodressed himself head to toe in armor. ThenHe Jinled more than thirty high officials into the palace one after the other, includingHe Yong,Xun You, andZheng Tai. They stood before the coffin of Emperor Ling and brought forward Liu Bian to acclaim him as the newEmperor. After the ministers had finished their ceremonies,Yuan Shaoled his troops into the inner palace to arrestJian Shuo;Jian Shuofled in fright to hide himself under the plants in the palace garden, but he was discovered by one ofThe Ten,Guo Sheng, who killed him. All of the palace guards who had been underJian Shuo’s command threw down their weapons andsubmitted.2223•••

Of the three allies ofHe Jin, we metHe Yongin chapter one. Xun You was of a noted clan of scholars,Zheng Taiwas from a wealthy family and a later of the north, all three were personal followers ofHe Jin↩

Guo Shengcame from the same home area as the He clan and had been a close ally for some time.↩

Jian Shou wasHe Jin’s opponent and protector ofLiu Xie. Noted for his strength, energy and strategic ability, he was placed in charge of a new army corp as a counter-balance to the He’s and was evenHe Jin’s superior. His death removed a military and political threat toHe Jin

Yuan ShaoadvisedHe Jin, “The eunuchsare all allies in their own faction. We ought to exploit this opportunity to put all of them to death.” ButZhang Rangand the rest, knowing the danger they were in, fled in panic to appeal toEmpress He. They told her, “Jian Shuowas the only one who devised a plan to get rid of the General; none of us were involved in his plot. Yet the General has heeded the words ofYuan Shaoand means to execute us all. We implore you, Madam, take pity on us!”

Empress Dowager He said, “You all do not need to worry. I will protect you.”

She sent for her brother, and told him, “You and I came from meager origins; if not forZhang Rangand his friends, how could we have ever enjoyed such wealth and prestige?Jian Shuomay have been malicious, but he has already been put to death. Why do you place such faith in the words of this man, that you intend to execute all of the eunuchs?”

AndHe Jinobeyed her wish. He came out and told the soldiers and officials, “SinceJian Shuoplotted to kill me, you may wipe out his entire family. But there is no need to rashly harm the rest of the eunuchs.”

Yuan Shaosaid, “If you do not slay them, root and branch, they will be your downfall.”

ButHe Jinonly said, “I have decided; say no more.” And the soldiers and officials all withdrew.

The following day, the new Empress Dowager He appointedHe JinasAuthority over the Secretariat, affording him control over decrees and orders and placing him in the center of power. His companions were granted ranks and titles aswell.24•••

He Jin’s position gave him control of the Secretariat so control of decrees and orders, putting him in the center of power.↩

Empress Dowager Dong summonedZhang Rangand his party into her own palace for a council. Said she, “It was I who first brought forward the sister ofHe Jin. Today, her son is on the throne and all the ministers and officials are her confidantes. What can I do in the face of such overwhelming power?”

Zhang Rangreplied, “Madam, you should personally conduct court meetings and determine government affairs from ‘behind the screens’. You should appointLiu Xieas a prince, you should grantState UncleDong Chongsome great office and place military authority in his hands, and you should continue to employ our aid. By doing these things, you may yet accomplish the greataffair.”25•••

Dong Chongwas Dowager Dong’s nephew via her disgraced elder brother.↩

Empress Dowager Dong was very pleased with these suggestions; at the court assembly the following day, she issued a decree appointingLiu XieasPrinceofChenliuandDong ChongasGeneral of Coursers, whileZhang Rangand the others participated in government affairs. Learning that her rival was thus seeking to control power, Empress Dowager He prepared a feast within the palace and invited Empress Dowager Dong to attend. In the middle of the feast, when all were well warmed with wine, she rose and offered a toast to her guest, bowing and saying, “We are but women, after all; meddling in government affairs does not suit us. When Empress Lü once sought to grasp all power over the state, in the end it brought about the deaths of the thousand members of her clan. We ought to remain content, deeply secluded within the ‘nine gates’ of our palaces, and leave state affairs to the statesmen; that would be a blessing for the state. I hope you will be so gracious as to consider mywords.”26•••

Empress Lü had been wife to the founder of theFormer Hanand mother of the second Emperor, Dowager to three. She ruled China as Dowager and when she died, her family was accused of plotting to overthrow theHan. She ended up being used as a warning against women having too much power.↩

Yet Empress Dowager Dong only raged, “You poisoned Beauty Wang to death out of your own jealousy, and now that your son sits on the throne and your brotherHe Jinis powerful, you think you can say whatever nonsense you like! I could order theGeneral of Coursersto cut off your brother’s head with just a flick of the wrist!”

Empress Dowager Hefumed, “I tried to persuade you with fair words; why get so angry?”

Dowager Dong said, “You are just a low-born daughter of a butcher! What do you know about anything?”

And the quarrel between the two ladies waxed hot.

Zhang Rangand the others persuaded the ladies to retire back to their palaces. But in the night,Empress Dowager Hesummoned her brother into the palace and told him what had occurred. He went out and took counsel with the principal officers of state. The next morning, a court assembly was held and a memorial was presented saying that in light of the fact that Empress Dowager Dong had never been the Empress, only having received her title asEmpress Dowagerwhen her son Emperor Ling was chosen to inherit the throne, it was no longer suitable for her to remain within the palace complex; she was to be relocated to her fief inHejian commanderyand was to depart the palace by the end of the day. Some troops were sent to evict and escort her away, while others were assigned to surround the office of her brotherDong Chongand to bring back his seal and ribbon of office. Knowing this was the end,Dong Chongslit his throat in the rear chamber; only once the guards heard the wailing of his household did theydisperse.27•••

Dowager Dong was unusual for a Han Dowager in having not been the wife of an Emperor and it provided the He’s an excuse to move her using old precedent. It was also unusual to have two Dowager’s, where both could form a political power base↩

For their part, seeing that their perch with Empress Dowager Dong had snapped,Zhang RangandDuan Guisent gifts of gold, pearls, and fine trinkets to form ties withHe Jin’s younger brotherHe Miaoand with their mother theLady Wuyang. Thus won over, the eunuchs had them go into the palace day and night to speak with Empress Dowager He and put in a good word for the eunuchs to hide their faults. By such measures, theTen Regular Attendantswere able to remain close to and favored by those in power.

In the sixth month,He Jinsecretly sent an agent who poisoned Empress Dowager Dong to death at a travel station inHejian. Her remains were brought to the capital and buried atWen Tombs.He Jinfeigned illness and did not attend thefuneral.•••

Fair to say, killing the mother of the late Emperor a few months after his death was not a great look for the new regime.

Yuan Shaowent one day to seeHe Jinand told him, “Zhang Rang,Duan Gui, and the rest of them are spreading rumors that you had Empress Dowager Dong poisoned and mean to launch a coup. Unless you seize this opportunity to execute the eunuchs, you will suffer greatly before long.Dou Wutoo once sought to execute the palace eunuchs, but his secret got out and he was the one who suffered the calamity instead. The generals and officials serving under you and your brother are all men of boldness and skill; if you would only use them to the fullest, everything would be in the palms of your hands. This is a heaven-sent opportunity; you cannot squanderit.”28•••

At the start of a young Emperor Ling’s reign in 168, Regent MarshalDou Wuand allies sought to remove the eunuch leaders of their time. The plot was slow going, the intent ofDou Wuhad became obvious and the plotters were lax.Dou Wuand co were destroyed in one night and there were repercussions for their supporters in the years ahead↩

ButHe Jinonly replied, “Let us discuss this with the others first.”

His servants secretly informedZhang Rang, whose faction sent word toHe Miaoin turn along with generous gifts and bribes.He Miaothus went in to speak with his sister and said, “The General is the chief supporter of the new Emperor, yet he is not gracious and merciful but thinks only of slaughter. Now he wants to slay theTen Regular Attendantswithout any reason. This would only incite disorder.” Empress Dowager He took his advice to heart.

Soon after,He Jinentered and told her of his design to put the eunuchs to death. She argued, “It is establishedHanpractice that such palace officials manage and supervise the inner apartments. And thoughHis Late Majestyhas only just taken his leave of the realm, you want to kill and execute his old ministers; that would demean the dignity of the ancestral temple.” True to his indecisive nature,He Jinagreed with her, muttering approval as he left.

Yuan Shaoconfronted him, asking, “How do things stand?”

He Jinsaid, “TheEmpress Dowageris against it. What can I do now?”

Yuan Shaosaid, “You could summon bold fellows from across the realm and have them bring their troops to the capital to execute all of the eunuchs. Under such pressing circumstances, we cannot let the disapproval of theEmpress Dowagerstop us.”

He Jinsaid, “A fine plan indeed!”

And he was just about to send out orders to every garrison to command leaders to march onthe capital.

But theMaster of Records,Chen Lin, said, “Absurd! The proverbial fool ‘covers his eyes to better catch the swallows’. You would only be deceiving yourself! Even with petty matters, you cannot be deceived just to achieve your goals, and what of imperial affairs? General, you already wield imperial authority and have soldiers at hand: you soar like a dragon and prowl like a tiger and you determine the prominence of others at your own discretion, and if you wanted to execute the eunuchs it would be as simple as roasting hairs in the heat of a furnace. You need only resolve to do the deed and then swiftly execute it, and both Heaven and the people would go along with it. Why then should you instead send out declarations to the generals of the realm to infringe upon the capital and violate the palace, gathering bold and brash men together each with their own ambitions? That would only be what they call ‘laying aside your own weapon and giving the handle to another’. Although you could not help but succeed, it would only produce further turmoil.”

He Jinlaughed and said, “The view of a merecoward!”29•••

Arrogant men dismissing cautious warnings from wise scholars as lacking boldness is a theme in the novel.↩

He Jinis dithering about what to do with the eunuchs, swayed at one moment but his supporters and other times by his sister, but also rude to scholarly men who warn him of dangers. Not a man to admire

Then one of those about him suddenly clapped his hands and laughed, exclaiming, “This is all as easy as turning over one’s hand! Why so much talk?”

They turned and saw that it wasCao Cao.

And so it was:

If you want to purge the villains from the land

You had better heed a cunning man

To know the wordsCao Caowill speak, read the next chapter to find out.
